lesson.big_idea: Water travels the Earth in a continuous cycle — evaporating, condensing, and falling again — without being used up.

sections.title: Follow One Drop
sections.body: Have you ever wondered where rain comes from after a sunny morning? Follow one drop of water around the Earth.

sections.title: Using the Diagram
sections.body: The cycle diagram shows evaporation, condensation, precipitation, and collection. Trace the arrows so the journey feels clear.

sections.title: Concept: Water Cycle
sections.body: The water cycle is a continuous process. Water changes form, but the amount of water on Earth stays in balance.

sections.title: Process Stages
sections.body: Heat lifts liquid water as vapour. Cooling forms clouds. Rain, snow, or hail return water to land and sea.

sections.title: Understanding Simply
sections.body: Sunlight evaporates water. Cool air condenses vapour into droplets. Precipitation brings water home again.

sections.title: In Real Life
sections.body: Steam above a hot cup is a tiny reminder of evaporation — like the cycle, only closer to your hands.

sections.title: Lesson Summary
sections.body: Evaporation, condensation, and precipitation keep water moving. You can explain one stage and feel ready to revise.

sections.title: Think About It
sections.body: Which stage have you seen this week — steam, clouds, or rain?

diagram_package.title: The Water Cycle
diagram_package.caption: Evaporation → Condensation → Precipitation → Collection
diagram_package.explanation: This diagram reduces overload by showing one clear loop.
diagram_package.practice_question: Point to condensation and say what is happening to the water.
